*Country:* ireland
*Region:* the heartlands

*Period:* 13th-19th century
*Coords:* 53.1, -7.7
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kinnitty_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Kinnitty%20Castle/@53.068,-7.72,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/IMG_KinnittyCastle0973.jpg/330px-IMG_KinnittyCastle0973.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* https://www.kinnittycastlehotel.com/ add source, tag as hotel 

*Description:*
Kinnitty Castle or Castle Bernard is a 19th-century gothic revival castle and hotel in Kinnitty, County Offaly, Ireland. It is located contiguous to Droughtville and Lettybrook, north of the Slieve Bloom Mountains on the R421 regional road between the villages of Kinnitty and Cadamstown. A nearby pyramid was built by the Bernard family who resided in the castle.

_Listing 3 of 53_

*Name:* Rathcroghan
*Country:* ireland
*Region:* ulster and the north
*County:* County Roscommon
*Type:* castle
*Period:* Bronze Age-Iron Age
*Coords:* 53.802, -8.304
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rathcroghan
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Rathcroghan/@53.8,-8.303,15z
*Main Image:* https://img.castlecore.uk/rathcroghan.jpg
*Gallery:* 4 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* more research 

*Description:*
Rathcroghan is a complex of archaeological sites near Tulsk in County Roscommon, Ireland. It is identified as the site of Cruachan, the traditional capital of the Connachta, the prehistoric and early historic rulers of the western territory. The Rathcroghan Complex is an archaeological landscape with many references found in early Irish medieval manuscripts.

_Listing 4 of 53_

*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* https://abbeyofdonegal.ie/ add source

*Description:*
Donegal Abbey is a ruined Franciscan Priory in Donegal in Ireland. It was constructed by the O'Donnell dynasty in the fifteenth century and remained a center of Classical Christian education even after its destruction during the Nine Years War. It is sometimes referred to as Donegal Friary.

_Listing 5 of 53_

*Name:* Springhill House
*Country:* ireland
*Region:* ulster and the north
*County:* County Derry
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 17th century
*Coords:* 54.686, -6.656
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Springhill_House
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Springhill%20House/@54.747,-6.718,15z
*Main Image:* https://img.castlecore.uk/springhill-house.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Springhill is a 17th-century plantation house in the townland of Ballindrum near Moneymore, County Londonderry in Northern Ireland. It has been the property of the National Trust since 1957 and, in addition to the house, gardens and park, there is a costume collection.
It is open from March to June, and September on weekends, and is open to the public seven days a week during July and August.

_Listing 7 of 53_

*Description:*
Dunseverick Castle is built of basalt and is situated in County Antrim, Northern Ireland, near the small village of Dunseverick and the Giant's Causeway. Dunseverick Castle and earthworks are Scheduled Historic Monuments in the townland of Feigh, in Causeway coast and Glens district council, at grid ref: C9871 4467.

*Name:* Abbey Knockmoy
*Country:* ireland
*Region:* wild atlantic way
*County:* County Galway
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 12th century
*Coords:* 53.47, -8.62
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Knockmoy_Abbey
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Abbey%20Knockmoy/@53.47,-8.62,15z
*Main Image:* https://img.castlecore.uk/abbey-knockmoy.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* https://maps.app.goo.gl/7xNzKjV54zGGn1aD6 update google places link 


*Description:*
Abbeyknockmoy is a village and parish in County Galway, Ireland. It is known for the nearby ruins of the 12th century Cistercian abbey, established with the Kings of Connacht as its benefactors. The abbey was the burial site of King Cathal Crobhdearg Ua Conchobair and contains several examples of medieval wall paintings and sculpture. It was formerly part of the kingdom of the Soghain of Connacht.

_Listing 9 of 53_

*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* no
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Dune is a science fiction media franchise that originated with the 1965 novel of the same name by American author Frank Herbert. Dune is frequently cited as the best-selling science fiction novel in history, and won the 1966 Hugo Award as well as the inaugural Nebula Award for Best Novel. Herbert wrote five sequels before his death in 1986: Dune Messiah (1969), Children of Dune (1976), God Emperor of Dune (1981), Heretics of Dune (1984), and Chapterhouse: Dune (1985).

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Wolvesey Castle
*Country:* england
*Region:* south east london
*County:* Hampshire
*Type:* palace
*Period:* 12th century
*Coords:* 51.0592, -1.31
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wolvesey_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Wolvesey%20Castle/@51.0572,-1.3214,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZODBkrIQhHnnSuVTk3mmLUtZOIiKw6Dv1hNugEX0RTUP0qkm0-HhoVkvBykjDMmsNdG7wFAmLD7JTRqSKjFhFj6_cfv9IGPudgzyOXhoBOliMti7W9_6aBb86GjQnVchw8sWmAP0t0QCBeVLYw=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Wolvesey Castle, in Winchester, Hampshire, England, was the main residence of the Bishop of Winchester in the Middle Ages. The castle, mostly built by Henry of Blois in the 12th century, is now a ruin, except for its fifteenth-century chapel, which is now part of the bishop's current residence, Wolvesey Palace. Wolvesey Castle was primarily a palace, although Blois had it fortified because of the Anarchy.

_Listing 12 of 53_

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Reading Abbey
*Country:* england
*Region:* south east london
*County:* Berkshire
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 12th century
*Coords:* 51.45638889, -0.965
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reading_Abbey
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Reading%20Abbey/@51.4536,-0.9683,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZPA9DLMTI9QaJ7PHzA1_B5Vy6675EnnPlNVTIeJlGDi8pjUAZ8EksmX-xW_AjbYx4gtLfIEoJOxePpLgb_g-EUN5gRWVk5Yeb9KfZ9iwz4MdOMhE0etxgRa0Pkx1nJy8C9gyaL9KvR_Krs-VQ=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* update google places - https://maps.app.goo.gl/8diBkMHQ8xEsdrxw8Z


*Description:*
Reading Abbey is a large, ruined abbey in the centre of the town of Reading, in the English county of Berkshire. It was founded by Henry I in 1121 "for the salvation of my soul, and the souls of King William, my father, and of King William, my brother, and Queen Maud, my wife, and all my ancestors and successors." In its heyday the abbey was one of Europe's largest royal monasteries. The traditions of the Abbey are continued today by the neighbouring St James's Church, which is partly built using stones of the Abbey ruins.

_Listing 13 of 53_

listing.name: Reading Abbey
listing.desc: Reading Abbey is a large, ruined abbey in the centre of the town of Reading, in the English county of Berkshire. It was founded by Henry I in 1121 "for the salvation of my soul, and the souls of King William, my father, and of King William, my brother, and Queen Maud, my wife, and all my ancestors and successors." In its heyday the abbey was one of Europe's largest royal monasteries. The traditions of the Abbey are continued today by the neighbouring St James's Church, which is partly built using stones of the Abbey ruins.

imageAttribution.description: The ruins of the monks dormitory of Reading Abbey, in the English town of Reading. For more information, see Wikipedia article Reading Abbey.
imageAttribution.credit: Photograph taken by chris_j_wood on the 9th February 2005, with original filename DCP_3628.jpg. Cross-loaded from wikipedia-en.

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[🔧 STILL IN PROGRESS\]

*Name:* Taunton Castle
*Country:* england
*Region:* south west
*County:* Somerset
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 12th-17th century
*Coords:* 51.0158, -3.1046
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taunton_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Taunton%20Castle/@51.0178,-3.1014,15z
*Main Image:* none
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* unknown
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* needs more research 

*Description:*
Taunton Castle is a castle built to defend the town of Taunton, Somerset, England. It has origins in the Anglo Saxon period and was later the site of a priory. The Normans then built a stone structured castle, which belonged to the Bishops of Winchester. The current heavily reconstructed buildings are the inner ward, which now houses the Museum of Somerset and the Somerset Military Museum. The building was designated a grade I listed building in 1952.

_Listing 14 of 53_

The current heavily reconstructed buildings are the inner ward, which now houses the Museum of Somerset and the Somerset Military Museum. The building was designated a grade I listed building in 1952.
